hero.kicker: An interactive impact story by the Educated Choices Program
hero.lead: Most were never taught what that choice does — to their body, to the planet, to the animals we share it with. This is the story of what happens when someone finally teaches them.
hero.eachDot: Every particle on this screen represents learners who watched an ECP lesson.

gap.title: Thousands of choices, zero lessons.
gap.step1: Before graduating, a young person will make roughly 15,000 food decisions — breakfast, lunch, snacks, dinner. Every single day.
gap.step2: Yet food's impact on health, climate, and animals is barely taught in school. In its place: marketing, habit, and a rising tide of online misinformation.
gap.step3: The result is a gap — between what people want to know about their food and the trustworthy facts available to them.
gap.step4: Closing that gap early, with science instead of judgment, is the entire mission of the Educated Choices Program.
gap.trayCaption: One lunch tray. One decision. Multiplied by millions of students, every day.

spark.title: It started with one person, in one classroom.
spark.intro: In 2015, Lorena Mucke began presenting food education in local Atlanta schools. No office. No staff. A simple belief: education is the most powerful tool for change.
spark.chartNote: Trajectory reconstructed from reported milestones (2024 and 2026 figures as reported by ECP).

founding.title: The first classroom
founding.text: Lorena Mucke starts presenting in Atlanta schools, one class at a time.

global.title: Going global
global.text: Free digital presentations carry the program far beyond Georgia — no cost, no barriers, just internet access.

library.title: The library opens
library.text: An on-demand video library launches, putting every education package one click away, 24/7.

study.title: Science checks the work
study.text: Researchers at the Universities of Edinburgh and Cambridge independently test whether ECP presentations change real food choices.

decade.title: A decade of transformation
decade.text: Ten years in: 21+ education packages, Spanish-language expansion, and a new series for learners aged 8–11.

today.title: 3.79 million and counting
today.text: From one person to a global program — still free, still science-based, still growing.

spark.statPackagesNote: videos, lesson plans, quizzes & more — always free
spark.statLanguagesNote: narration & captions, from Spanish to Hindi

world.title: From Atlanta to 77 countries and territories.
world.step1: It spread across the Americas first — from the United States and Canada down through Mexico, Central America, and nearly every country in South America.
world.step2: Then Europe and Africa: classrooms in Spain, Poland, and Ukraine; communities in Kenya, Nigeria, and The Gambia.
world.step3: Then Asia and Oceania — India, Japan, Indonesia, Australia. In Q1 2026 alone, Slovakia, Lebanon, and Greece became countries #72, #73, and #74.
world.step4: Today, ECP materials are used in 77 countries and territories, in English and Spanish with captions in 10+ languages. Anyone, anywhere, can book them for free.

proof.title: Don't take their word for it. Take the data.
proof.expIntro: In 2024, researchers ran a real-world experiment at the University of Edinburgh.
proof.expStep1: Students attended a presentation. Half saw ECP's "The Planet and Our Plates". The other half — the control group — saw a presentation with nothing to do with food.
proof.expStep2: Afterwards, every student got a voucher for a free meal at a local café. What they didn't know: their meal choices were being recorded.
proof.expStep3: The students who saw the ECP presentation chose meat-free meals almost 2.5 times as often. The difference was statistically significant.
proof.expSource: Real food choice study by Bryant Research at the University of Edinburgh, n=39. A parallel study at the University of Cambridge also found statistically significant effects.
proof.gaugeLabel: of viewers intend to make plant-forward dietary changes after watching
proof.gaugeNote: n={{n}} surveyed between Feb 2024 and Mar 2026 · excludes viewers already fully plant-based
proof.multiplierLabel: increase in participants choosing plant-forward options after using ECP materials, in a recent study
proof.whyIntro: When viewers explain their motivation, three reasons lead — the same three pillars in ECP's seal: personal health, the planet, and animals.

barriers.title: …and the barriers that remain
barriers.taste: I like the taste of meat

proof.barriersNote: Survey of 371 viewers, 2024 Annual Report. Knowing the barriers is how ECP designs what comes next.

ripple.title: One plate seems small. Millions are not.
ripple.intro: When 90% of 3.7 million learners intend to eat differently, the ripples reach the atmosphere.
ripple.counterLabel: kg of CO₂ emissions prevented every year
ripple.counterNote: Estimated using diet impact statistics from Scarborough et al., 2014
ripple.equivIntro: Every single year, that is roughly the same as…
ripple.closing: No fleet was grounded and no forest was planted. People simply learned — and chose differently.

voices.title: Six months later, they still remember.
voices.intro: ECP follows up with learners long after the classroom lights come back on. This is what they say.

power.text: It reframes personal power. Instead of feeling overwhelmed by global problems, you suddenly see that your daily habits actually matter. You don't walk away with guilt — you walk away with options.

pollution.text: I didn't really know that raising animals for food creates pollution. I also now know that food can help or hurt my body.

lessMeat.text: One thing I changed as a result of what I saw is eating less meat and trying more plant-based foods.

wholefoods.text: One thing I changed is WHOLE FOODS! Consuming as much whole foods as possible.

redMeat.text: I've started to cut back on red meat and focused more on fruits and vegetables.

teacher.text: I thought the material was very professional and visually appealing. The students were engaged and it was a valuable lesson about the quality of food.

stewardship.title: Where a donated dollar actually goes.
stewardship.intro: A fully remote team of just 11 people runs a program reaching millions — and opens its books to prove it.
stewardship.centsLabel: of every dollar spent in 2024 went directly to education programs
stewardship.costLabel: ≈ cost to reach one learner (Q1 2026 expenses ÷ reported learners)
stewardship.badgesTitle: Trusted by the people who rate nonprofits

badges.bestCharities: America's Best Charities — Seal of Excellence

donate.title: The next 3.7 million learners are waiting.
donate.lead: ECP is funded entirely by donations and private grants. Every education package is free for every classroom on Earth — because people like you pay it forward.
donate.tierNote: Estimates based on Q1 2026 expenses (~${{cost}} per learner reached). Every contribution of any size helps.

t1.label: brings a full classroom of 30 students their first food-impact lesson

t2.label: reaches 10 classrooms — about 300 students — with science-based food education

t3.label: powers an entire school's worth of learners, with lesson plans in two languages

donate.closing1: Ten years ago, this story was one person and one classroom.
donate.closing2: The next chapter is written by whoever turns knowledge into action.
donate.closing3: That can be you. Right now.
donate.orgLine: Educated Choices Program is a registered 501(c)(3) nonprofit. EIN: 47-5137685.
